name: ALPR documentation: README + docs/ALPR.md + DEPLOYMENT.md
summary: Standalone documentation feature so the user (and curious onlookers, given this repo is MIT-licensed and public-friendly) can understand what the system does and doesn't do, the privacy/legal posture, the threat model it serves, and how to enable + disable cleanly. Mirrors the depth of docs/DEPLOYMENT.md and docs/DEVICE-SETUP.md. Lives primarily in a new docs/ALPR.md plus surgical updates to README.md and docs/DEPLOYMENT.md. Avoid restating low-level acceptance criteria from other features -- this doc is the user-facing surface.
acceptance_criteria: New file docs/ALPR.md with these sections (in order): (1) What this feature does (and explicitly: does NOT do): defensive personal-safety stalking detection on the user's own footage; not surveillance, not LE-style ALPR, not networked. Single-user, single-server, zero outbound by default. (2) Threat model: who this protects against (someone following the user across trips with the same vehicle), who it does not (a state-level adversary; a local attacker who has root + the encryption key; a determined adversary who switches vehicles every trip; etc.). Be honest about limitations. (3) Privacy & legal posture: encryption-at-rest, retention defaults, no upload, no networking outside the engine sidecar, jurisdictional notes (Illinois BIPA, EU GDPR, UK DPA, Canada PIPEDA -- all with a hard 'this is not legal advice; consult counsel before recording in your jurisdiction' disclaimer). (4) How it works: the offline pipeline (frame -> engine -> detection -> encounter -> heuristic -> alert), with a small ASCII architecture diagram. (5) Hardware requirements: CPU vs GPU benchmarks from the spike, RAM headroom, disk-impact estimate (~MB per route at default fps). (6) Enabling step-by-step: (a) generate encryption key with `go run ./cmd/alpr-keygen`, (b) put in .env as ALPR_ENCRYPTION_KEY, (c) restart server, (d) Settings -> Optional services -> ALPR -> Enable, (e) ack disclaimer, (f) `make alpr-up`. (7) Disabling cleanly: Settings toggle off (workers stop, APIs gate-503, UI surfaces vanish) + `make alpr-down` (frees container resources). Mention that data is retained until cleanup runs or the user runs the wipe SQL snippet documented in section 8. (8) Optional data wipe: SQL one-liner to truncate ALPR tables for users who want to scrub the DB. Warn that this is irreversible. (9) Operational tips: how to spot OCR drift (sample plate-hash distribution), when to re-tune (false positives from common neighbors, missed real plates), log signals to watch (alpr_engine_errors_total, alpr_dropped_no_gps_total). (10) FAQ: 'Why are some plates always missed?' (low light, motion blur, dirty plates), 'Why did my own car alert?' (shouldn't normally; whitelist it), 'Can I export blurred video?' (yes; see redaction settings on share + export), 'Is this legal where I live?' (depends; consult counsel; this is not legal advice), 'What if I rotate the encryption key?' (all prior plate data is unreadable; document the rotation cost loudly), 'Can I run this without Docker?' (yes -- docker is the default but the engine is just an HTTP service; users can run it bare-metal pointing ALPR_ENGINE_URL at any compatible service).

README.md updates: (1) 'What it does' bullet added: 'Optional license-plate stalking detection -- opt-in feature that processes dashcam frames locally to warn when the same vehicle appears across multiple trips. Off by default. See docs/ALPR.md.' (2) New 'Optional services' subsection or paragraph in Setup that names ALPR + makes it explicit it's off by default and how to enable. (3) Environment-variable table gains the ALPR-related vars (ALPR_ENGINE_URL, ALPR_ENCRYPTION_KEY, ALPR_REGION, ALPR_FPS, ALPR_CONFIDENCE_MIN, ALPR_RETENTION_DAYS_*, ALPR_NOTIFY_*); each with a short description and a pointer to docs/ALPR.md for the full picture.

README.md API table gains the new ALPR endpoints under a new 'ALPR (optional)' section: GET/PUT /v1/settings/alpr, POST /v1/settings/alpr/disclaimer/ack, GET /v1/routes/:dongle/:route/plates, GET /v1/plates/:hash, GET/POST /v1/alpr/alerts, POST /v1/alpr/alerts/:hash/ack|unack|note, GET/POST/DELETE /v1/alpr/whitelist, GET /v1/alpr/alerts/summary, PATCH /v1/alpr/detections/:id, POST /v1/alpr/plates/merge, POST/GET /v1/alpr/backfill/*, POST /v1/alpr/heuristic/reevaluate, POST /v1/alpr/notify/test, GET /v1/routes/:dongle/:route/turns. One row per endpoint, matching the existing table format.

docs/DEPLOYMENT.md updates: short 'ALPR sidecar' subsection covering -- GPU passthrough notes (uncommenting the device_requests stanza in docker-compose.yml, hardware checks), network isolation (sidecar binds compose-network only by default; do NOT publish 8081 in prod), backup considerations (the encryption key is critical -- losing it loses all plate data, treat ALPR_ENCRYPTION_KEY like a database password). Cross-link to docs/ALPR.md for non-deployment topics.
docs/index.html (if it exists; check) gets a link to ALPR.md in its docs index.

CONTRIBUTING.md updates (if file exists): brief note that the ALPR engine container is excluded from CI builds by default (because it pulls a multi-GB ML image), with the contributor opt-in instructions (`make alpr-build` locally).
All Markdown lints pass: consistent heading levels, no broken intra-doc links, no orphaned section headings.
Cross-references: every settings key, env var, and endpoint named in docs/ALPR.md must match the canonical names in the actual implementation features. Do a grep pass before merging to catch drift.

Tone: factual, non-alarmist, non-promotional. The user is enabling a tool; the docs help them use it responsibly. Avoid marketing language ('powerful AI-driven', 'seamlessly integrated'); prefer concrete description ('processes ~2 frames per second per route at default settings; ~30 ms per frame on a mid-range CPU').
